right IJ, all unchanged in position compared to the previous study.
The nasogastric tube is seen coiling above the diaphragm consistent

middle and lower lung zone opacities consistent with infection,

fracture on the left.

CLINICAL HISTORY:  A 69-year-old male with history of pancreatic 
mass.  
 
COMPARISON:  4/23/2004
 
FINDINGS:  Single frontal view of the chest obtained 04-23 at 
0036 hours demonstrates stable positioning of the support equipment.  
The left upper extremity PICC remains curled, likely within the 
azygos vein.  The side-port of the NG tube remains near the distal 
esophagus.  Cardiomediastinal silhouette is stable in configuration 
with mild cardiomegaly.  There has been interval increase in 
bilateral pleural effusions and bibasilar opacities.  Diffuse 
reticular and airspace opacities may reflect moderate edema.  
 
Subsequent single frontal view of the chest obtained 4/23/2004 at 
0534 hours demonstrates interval improved aeration of the lung bases.  
No other significant interval change.  Support equipment remains in 
unchanged position.  
 
IMPRESSION: 
 
1.  SERIES OF TWO CHEST RADIOGRAPHS DEMONSTRATE INTERVAL INCREASE IN 
BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS.  INCREASING BIBASILAR OPACITIES WITH 
DIFFUSE RETICULAR AND AIRSPACE OPACITIES MAY REFLECT MODERATE 
PULMONARY EDEMA.  STABLE CONFIRMATION OF THE CARDIOMEDIASTINAL 
SILHOUETTE WITH MILD CARDIOMEGALY.  
 
2.  SUPPORT EQUIPMENT REMAINS IN UNCHANGED POSITION, WITH THE LEFT 
UPPER EXTREMITY PICC LIKELY IN THE AZYGOS VEIN, AND THE NG SIDE-PORT 
NEAR THE DISTAL ESOPHAGUS, AS DESCRIBED ABOVE.

PORTABLE SEMIUPRIGHT CHEST:  4/16/2020
 
COMPARISON:  Compared with the prior from 4/16/2020. 
 
CLINICAL HISTORY:  This is a 97-four-year-old male with chest 
pain. 
 
FINDINGS:  The patient is again noted to be status post median 
sternotomy with sternal wires as well as circular markers within the 
mediastinum.  There has been interval placement of a left subclavian 
central venous catheter, which terminates at the junction of the left 
subclavian and superior vena cava.  
 
The right hemidiaphragm is again noted to be elevated with 
obscuration of the lateral margin, which may be due to pleural 
effusion.  There is less atelectasis at the right lung base.  
Overall, the lungs appear more aerated.  
 
The cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits of size.  
Again seen are calcifications of the aorta. 
 
The osseous structures appear unchanged. 
 
IMPRESSION: 
 
1.  NEW LEFT SUBCLAVIAN CENTRAL VENOUS CATHETER, TERMINATING AT THE 
SUBCLAVIAN/SUPERIOR VENA CAVAL JUNCTION.

MARGIN, RAISING THE POSSIBILITY OF A PLEURAL EFFUSION.  STABLE 
COMPARED WITH 20/16.

report:
NARRATIVE:
RADIOGRAPHIC EXAMINATION OF THE CHEST: 5-21-2010
 
CLINICAL HISTORY: 44 years of age, Female, Portable upright, sob, r/o 
pna, ptx.
 
COMPARISON: 5/21/10 and 21st May 10
PROCEDURE COMMENTS: Single view of the chest. 
 
FINDINGS:
 
There are low lung volumes. There is increased patchy opacity at the 
right lung base and in the upper perimediastinal regions bilaterally 
with prominent bronchopulmonary vascular markings. Findings could be 
suggestive of pneumonia or edema, especially with the findings of 
mild peribronchial thickening. Cardiac size is unchanged and slightly 
enlarged. No change in right pacemaker.
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  Low lung volumes and increased patchy opacities suggestive of 
pneumonia or edema, probably accentuated by the low lung volumes.

report:
NARRATIVE:
CHEST AND PELVIS: 3-21-18.
COMPARISON:
None.
FINDINGS:
Chest: A single AP portable supine view was obtained at 1026 hours
on a trauma board. The cardiomediastinal size and configuration
are within normal limits. Dense opacity is seen in the right upper
lung zone, with patchy opacity also seen in the left upper lung
zone. While these findings may be due to overlying structures,it
is difficult to exclude pulmonary contusion. No obvious rib
fracture is identified. Patient is status post endotracheal
intubation, with the tip of the endotracheal tube demonstrated
approximately 4 cm above the carina. There is a fracture of the
distal third of the left clavicle, with inferior displacement of
the distal fragment.
Single portable supine view obtained at 1214 hours demonstrates
placement of a nasogastric tube, with tip not included on the
image, but extending at least to the level of the body of the
stomach. Endotracheal tube is again seen. There has also been
interval placement of a right chest drain, with side port
demonstrated within the thorax, and tip projecting toward the right
apex, but not included on the image. Patchy opacity is seen atthe
right lung base, with worsened right upper lung zone consolidation
and persistent streaky opacity at the left lung apex. A needlewas
seen projecting over the right lung apex, questionably relatedto
right lung decompression. Endotracheal tube is again seen, with
radiographically satisfactory position.
Pelvis: A frontal view of the pelvis was obtained on a trauma
board, with overlying artifact. No evidence is seen for definite
fracture or dislocation of the pelvic ring or visualized portions
of the proximal femora. Right femoral catheters are in place,
projecting to the level of the mid and upper sacrum, respectively.
There is an unremarkable bowel gas pattern.
IMPRESSION:
1. SEQUENCE OF CHEST RADIOGRAPHS DEMONSTRATING DENSE RIGHT UPPER
LUNG OPACITY AND STREAKY LEFT APICAL OPACITY, THAT COULD BE RELATED
TO PULMONARY CONTUSION. NO OBVIOUS RIB FRACTURE IDENTIFIED.
PLEASE REFER TO CT EXAMINATION PERFORMED THE SAME DAY FOR FURTHER
INFORMATION.
2. STATUS POST ENDOTRACHEAL INTUBATION, WITH THE TIP OF THE
ENDOTRACHEAL TUBE DEMONSTRATED APPROXIMATELY 4 CM ABOVE THE CARINA.
3. STATUS POST PLACEMENT OF RIGHT CHEST DRAIN AND NASOGASTRIC
TUBE.
4. NO DEFINITE FRACTURE OF THE PELVIS IDENTIFIED.
END OF IMPRESSION:
SUMMARY 4: Possible Significant Abnormality Change, may need
action.

02/29.

effusions bilaterally, may suggest mild progression of pulmonary 
edema.  Additionally cannot exclude infiltrate at the right lung base 
given more confluent opacity.

EXAM: Chest 2 Views 6/30/11
 
CLINICAL HISTORY: chest tightness with inspiration x 1 week
 
COMPARISON: No prior.
 
Findings:
 
The trachea is midline.  The cardiomediastinal silhouette is within 
normal limits.
 
The pulmonary vasculature is well defined.  No pulmonary edema.  
 
The costophrenic angles are sharp on the frontal radiograph of the 
chest.  Due to mild patient rotation on the lateral radiograph, 
evaluation of the left costophrenic angle is mildly limited.  No 
definite pleural effusion.
 
There is a 4-mm nodule overlying the right lung base which given its 
small size and density likely represents a calcified granuloma.  The 
lungs are otherwise clear.  No pulmonary mass or focal consolidation.
 
The visualized osseous structures are grossly unremarkable.
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.NO ACUTE CARDIOPULMONARY PROCESS.
 
2.MILDLY LIMITED EVALUATION OF THE COSTOPHRENIC ANGLES ON THE LATERAL 
RADIOGRAPH OF THE CHEST SECONDARY TO PATIENT ROTATION.  NO DEFINITE 
PLEURAL EFFUSION.
 
 
SUMMARY:1-NO SIGNIFICANT ABNORMALITY

report:
NARRATIVE:
CLINICAL HISTORY:   Kidney transplant candidate evaluation.
 FINDINGS:
 Two views of the chest 7/29/2007 are evaluated without prior
comparison study.  The heart appears moderately-to-markedly enlarged.
A left-sided central venous catheter with two lumens, likely a
hemodialysis catheter, is seen with distal tip at the level of the
right atrium.  There is perhaps minimal blunting of the costophrenic
sulci bilaterally.  There is no pneumothorax.  Pulmonary vasculature
appears slightly indistinct, particularly at the right mid to lower
lung zones.  There are no areas of dense parenchymal lobar
consolidation.  There is minimal fluid seen tracking in the right
major fissure and perhaps trace fluid at the right minor fissure.
 IMPRESSION:
 1.  CARDIOMEGALY.
 2.  TRACE PLEURAL EFFUSION AT THE RIGHT GREATER THAN LEFT LUNG BASE,
WITH SUGGESTION OF A FEW KERLEY B LINES AT THE RIGHT LUNG BASE WITH
SLIGHTLY INDISTINCT PULMONARY INTERSTITIAL MARKINGS.  THE
CONSTELLATION OF FINDINGS SUGGESTS SOME ELEMENT OF INTERSTITIAL
PULMONARY EDEMA, HOWEVER, AN ATYPICAL INFECTIOUS PROCESS OR OTHER
ETIOLOGY IS NOT EXCLUDED.  RECOMMEND CLINICAL CORRELATION AND
FOLLOW-UP IMAGING CHEST X-RAY IF CLINICALLY INDICATED.
 END OF IMPRESSION:
SUMMARY: 4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

diffuse groundglass and consolidative opacities throughout both 
lungs. Stable moderate bilateral pleural effusions. Apparent slight 
retraction of right internal jugular line may be due to differences 
in patient positioning. Feeding tube tip may be looped, with tip over 
the distal esophagus. Nasogastric tube tip is not well seen due to 
overlying hardware. New left internal jugular line, with tip 
overlying the left brachia cephalic vein. Left upper extremity PICC 
tip is not visualized due to overlying hardware.
 
Single frontal view chest 11/12/2002 at 0537: Right internal jugular 
line overlies the lower right internal jugular vein. Feeding tube tip 
overlies the distal esophagus. Left upper extremity PICC tip overlies 
the cavoatrial junction. Other support hardware are unchanged. Stable 
diffuse ground glass throughout both lungs; this has decreased 
slightly over the past two days, which may reflect decreased 
pulmonary edema. Stable small bilateral pleural effusions.
 
 
 
 
IMPRESSION:

WITH TIP OVER THE DISTAL ESOPHAGUS. ONE ENTERIC TUBE APPEARS LOOPED 
WITHIN THE MOUTH. NEW LEFT INTERNAL JUGULAR LINE, WITH TIP OVERLYING 
THE LEFT BRACHIOCEPHALIC VEIN. OTHER SUPPORT HARDWARE ARE STABLE IN 
POSITION.

report:
NARRATIVE:
CHEST ONE VIEW:  9-14-2015 
 
CLINICAL HISTORY:  Eighty-one-year-old man status post esophagectomy 
and pharyngectomy.  Referred for possible pneumothorax.  Additional 
film was obtained to evaluate positioning of a nasogastric tube.  
 
EXAM: 
1.  Single semiupright of the chest dated 9-14-15 at 1706 
hours. 
2.  Single upright view of the chest dated 9/14/2015 at 2216 
hours. 
 
COMPARISON:  None. 
 
FINDINGS:  Single semiupright view of the chest dated 9-14-2015 
at 1706 hours demonstrates a nasogastric tube placed.  However, the 
tip cannot be visualized below the left hemidiaphragm and can only be 
visualized overlying the lower thoracic spine and may be within the 
distal esophagus.  There is a cylindrical radiodensity projecting 
over the tracheal air stripe, which may be related to patient's 
recent postoperative state.  Lung volumes are low with dense 
retrocardiac air-space opacities some of appear linear in 
configuration as well as a probable small left pleural effusion.  No 
definite pneumothorax is appreciated on this semiupright view of the 
chest.  The cardiac silhouette is mildly enlarged.  There is a 
deformity of the left clavicle consistent with prior fracture 
deformity. 
 
Single upright view of the chest dated 2015/9/14 at 2216 hours 
demonstrates a nasogastric tube in place the tip of which projects to 
the gastric fundus.  The side port cannot be visualized.  Interval 
improved aeration of the retrocardiac air space with residual opacity 
likely representing atelectasis.  Probable small left pleural 
effusion.  The right lung remains clear.  No pneumothorax is 
appreciated.  A mask projects over the thoracic inlet.  
Redemonstration of cylindrical density over the tracheal air stripe.  
There is a rounded density projecting within the anterior aspect of 
the right second rib.  
 
IMPRESSION: 
 
1.  A SERIES OF TWO POSTOPERATIVE FILMS DEMONSTRATING A NASOGASTRIC 
TUBE WITH THE FINAL FILM DEMONSTRATING THE TIP PROJECTING TOWARDS THE 
GASTRIC FUNDUS. 
 
2.  NO EVIDENCE OF PNEUMOTHORAX.  
 
3.  RETROCARDIAC AIR-SPACE OPACITY LIKELY REPRESENTING ATELECTASIS IN 
THE POSTOPERATIVE SETTING.  PROBABLE SMALL LEFT PLEURAL EFFUSION.  
MILD CARDIOMEGALY. 
 
SUMMARY:4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

2O-7U-HZ-R1-2

report:
NARRATIVE:
CHEST X-RAY:  1/5/2015
COMPARISON:  1/5/2015.
CLINICAL INFORMATION:  Acute on chronic renal failure.  Short of
breath with blood transfusion.
IMPRESSION:
1.  AP SEMIERECT CHEST RADIOGRAPH DEMONSTRATES A LEFT-SIDED AICD
PACEMAKER, WITH THE PACING DEVICE NOT VISUALIZED ON THE FILM.
2.  STERNOTOMY WIRES ARE PRESENT, WITH ASSOCIATED MEDIASTINAL CLIPS.
THE HEART SIZE IS WITHIN NORMAL LIMITS.  STABLE CALCIFICATION IS SEEN
WITHIN THE AORTIC KNOB.
3.   MILD CEPHALIZATION IS PRESENT, WITH HAZY OPACIFICATION IN THE
MID AND LOWER ZONES BILATERALLY, CONCERNING FOR EARLY INTERSTITIAL
PULMONARY EDEMA.  THERE IS MORE FOCAL OPACIFICATION AT THE LEFT BASE,
WITH LIKELY SMALL BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS.
4.  STABLE ELEVATION OF THE LEFT HEMIDIAPHRAGM, WITH PROMINENT GAS
FILLED LOOPS OF COLON IN THE REGION OF THE SPLENIC FLEXURE.
5.  THERE IS ABNORMAL BONE DENSITY WITHIN THE LEFT HUMERAL HEAD, WITH
PROMINENT TRABECULATION AND MILD CORTICAL THICKENING.  THE
APPEARANCES ARE SUGGESTIVE OF UNDERLYING PAGET'S DISEASE.
SUMMARY:4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

NARRATIVE:
PORTABLE CHEST, SINGLE VIEW: 1/14/2005.
CLINICAL INFORMATION:
Patient with lymphoma. Intensive Care Unit admission. Please
evaluate.
COMPARISON:
1-14-2005.
FINDINGS:
There has been interval removal of the right subclavian catheter.
An endotracheal tube is present with the tip several centimeters
above the carina. Interval decrease in lung volumes. Interval
development of bilateral pleural effusions and reticular opacities
in both lungs. Changes are consistent with pulmonary edema.
Decreased aeration in both bases with bibasilar opacities. This
may represent atelectasis and/or pneumonia. There is no evidence
of pneumothorax.
IMPRESSION:
1. ENDOTRACHEAL TUBE PRESENT WITH TIP SEVERAL CENTIMETERS ABOVE
THE CARINA.
2. LOW LUNG VOLUMES.
3. MILD PULMONARY EDEMA.
4. BILATERAL PLEURAL EFFUSIONS.
5. BIBASILAR OPACITIES THAT MAY REPRESENT ATELECTASIS AND/OR
PNEUMONIA.
END OF IMPRESSION.

ACTION.

CLINICAL HISTORY: 75 years of age, Male, SOB.
 
COMPARISON: 8/6
 
PROCEDURE COMMENTS: Single view of the chest. 
 
FINDINGS:
 
2-lead cardiac pacemaker and mitral valvuloplasty are redemonstrated. 
Interval decrease in perihilar and right upper lung zone opacities, 
likely reflective of improving pulmonary edema. Bibasilar opacities, 
right greater than left, persists. A right pleural effusion is 
unchanged. No pneumothorax.

effect of mitral regurgitation.

as versus aspiration, or infectious consolidation.
 
4. Stable small right pleural effusion.

report:
NARRATIVE:
RADIOGRAPHIC EXAMINATION OF THE CHEST: 3/30/2001
 
CLINICAL HISTORY: 70 years of age, Male, Immunocompromised, LLL 
absence of breath sounds, evl for pneumonia/effusion.
 
COMPARISON: 3/30/2001, 01/30/03
 
PROCEDURE COMMENTS: Two views of the chest. 
 
FINDINGS:
 
There are airspace opacities and subsegmental consolidation in the 
retrocardiac portion of the left lower lobe as well as in the left 
perihilar region overall appearing similar to prior exam and 
suspicious for infection.
 
No pleural effusions.
 
Heart size normal and unchanged. The apparent right medial base 
opacity is most consistent with omental fat herniating into anterior 
mediastinum (more gagging hernia), better appreciated on 3/30/2001 
CT..
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  Persistent subsegmental consolidation in the left lung base and 
perihilar region, suspicious for infection.

report:
NARRATIVE:
CHEST, TWO VIEWS:  4-26-2002 
 
COMPARISON:  4-26-2002. 
 
CLINICAL HISTORY:  Seventy-three-year-old man with fever, cough.  
Patient with also history of rheumatoid arthritis.  
 
FINDINGS:  Frontal and lateral projections of the chest taken on 
26/4 at 1706 hours demonstrate a normal size cardiac 
silhouette.  Aorta is mildly tortuous.  There is a stent likely in 
the proximal left subclavian artery, unchanged from prior study.  
Again noted is diffuse peripheral reticular opacity in both lungs, 
not significantly changed since one-year ago.  This finding suggests 
pulmonary fibrosis.  There are cystic lucencies in both lungs 
diffusely distributed which may represent emphysema.  On the frontal 
projection, there is no acute change the previous study to suggest a 
pneumonia.  On the lateral projection, there is an area of increased 
density projecting over the ascending aorta, anterior to a major 
fissure.  This is new from prior study.  But in the absence of 
corroboration on the frontal view, this finding may be artifactual.
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  EVIDENCE FOR CHRONIC PULMONARY FIBROSIS AS WELL AS EMPHYSEMA.  
THIS APPEARS TO STABLE BE SINCE 4-26-02. 
 
2.  NO DEFINITE EVIDENCE FOR ACUTE PNEUMONIA BY COMPARING FRONTAL 
VIEW.  A NEW AREA OF ANTERIOR PULMONARY OPACITY SEEN ONLY ON THE 
LATERAL VIEW MAY BE ARTIFACTUAL.  HOWEVER, IF PATIENT'S SYMPTOMS 
PERSIST, RECOMMEND REPEAT CHEST RADIOGRAPHS TO CONFIRM.
 
SUMMARY:4-POSSIBLE SIGNIFICANT FINDINGS, MAY NEED ACTION

report:
NARRATIVE:
RADIOGRAPHIC EXAMINATION OF THE CHEST: 8-10-2002
 
CLINICAL HISTORY: 80 years of age, Male, Every morning while 
intubated.
 
COMPARISON: 8-10-2002
 
PROCEDURE COMMENTS: Single view of the chest. 
 
FINDINGS:
 
Interval extubation and removal of the enteric tube. New right-sided 
pleural drainage catheter placed. Other lines and support devices are 
stable including 2 right-sided central venous catheters, a 
mediastinal catheter, and a left-sided pleural catheter.
 
Visualization of the lung parenchyma is suboptimal secondary to 
rotary component of the patient. Increased left retrocardiac opacity 
in addition to interval development of more pronounced patchy 
opacities in the left lung base. Small bilateral pleural effusions, 
likely new in the right side.
 
Postoperative changes of the chest including sternotomy wires and 
several mediastinal clips, stable.
 
No acute osseous abnormalities. 
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  Interval extubation, removal of the enteric tube, and placement 
of a new right-sided pleural drainage catheter. Other lines and 
support devices are stable.
 
2.  Visualization of the lung parenchyma suboptimal secondary to 
rotary component of the patient. However, there is increased left 
retrocardiac opacity in addition to interval development of more 
pronounced patchy opacities in the left lung base. Findings may 
represent atelectasis or consolidation.
 
3.  Small bilateral pleural effusions, likely new in the right side.

NARRATIVE:
RADIOGRAPHIC EXAMINATION OF THE CHEST: 2016/8/23
 
CLINICAL HISTORY: 58 years of age, Male, Reevaluate multifocal 
pneumonia.
 
COMPARISON: 8/23/2016.
 
PROCEDURE COMMENTS: Two views of the chest. 
 
FINDINGS:
 
The mediastinal contour is normal. Aortic calcifications are noted. 
The heart is not enlarged. The pulmonary vessels are not distended. 
No evident pleural effusion or pneumothorax. There are patchy 
airspace opacities in both mid and lower lungs. Interstitial 
opacities are also seen in the mid and upper lungs. There is volume 
loss in the right middle lobe.
 
No significant osseous abnormalities are seen.
 
IMPRESSION:
 
1.  Unchanged patchy opacities in the bilateral mid and lower lungs 
concerning for pneumonia or aspiration.
